text: A 12mm working trocar is inserted in the left infraumbilical position along the medial clavicular line, and a 5mm trocar is placed on the epigastrium to the right of the midline. The optical trocar is inserted using an open technique in the infraumbilical position.

The surgeon uses an ultra shear to separate the omentum from the transverse colon, opening the omental bursa widely. This exposes the cystic tumor on the tail of the pancreas, measuring ten centimeters in diameter, and allows for the separation of its adhesions.

The surgeon identifies the corpus and parenchymal portions attached to the cystic wall. A flexible retractor is inserted through a five millimeter trocar to reposition the tumor, facilitating further dorsal dissection.
The surgeon carefully severs adhesions from the dorsal and retropancreatic directions, progressively disconnecting the tissue from the splenic hilus.

The surgeon identifies the splenic vein on the dorsal surface of the cyst and carefully severs the small branch veins between the pancreas and the splenic vein. The pancreatic branches and branches of the splenic artery are severed on the portion of the pancreas designated for removal, ensuring precise devascularization within the resection line.

The surgeon uses an endless stapler loaded with a musculoskeletal cartridge to safely resect the tail of the pancreas and the cystic tumor. Remaining small adhesions are severed using an ultra shear device.
The surgeon rinses and cleans the tumor bed, then seals the cut edge of the tail of the pancreas with fibrin adhesive to protect against fistula formation.
The surgeon removes the tumor through the infraumbilical trocar opening using an appropriately sized retrieval bag.
